my_sentence: Our country don't have enough meat.
correction: Our country doesn't have enough meat.
explanation: The singular subject "our country" takes "doesn't," not "don't." The teacher immediately recast the sentence with "doesn't."
correct_examples: Our country doesn't produce enough beef.
The company doesn't import meat.

my_sentence: That's a lot agricultural products.
correction: There are a lot of agricultural products.
explanation: Use "a lot of" before a plural countable noun. A plural subject also needs "there are," not "that's."
correct_examples: There are a lot of agricultural products.
China imports a lot of different products.

my_sentence: I usually buy some breads in Japan.
correction: I usually buy some bread in Japan.
explanation: "Bread" is normally uncountable, so it has no plural form when referring to the food generally. The teacher explicitly said there is no plural form here.
correct_examples: I usually buy some bread in Japan.
We bought two loaves of bread.

my_sentence: I remembered I buy some bread in China.
correction: I remember buying some bread in China.
explanation: For a present memory of a past action, use present "remember" followed by a gerund: "remember buying."
correct_examples: I remember buying bread there.
She remembers visiting that bakery.

my_sentence: It's difficult to importer them from Japan.
correction: It's difficult to import them from Japan.
explanation: After "to," use the base verb "import." "Importer" is a noun for a person or company that imports goods.
correct_examples: It is difficult to import seafood.
The importer buys products from Japan.
context: Seafood imports during a trade dispute.

my_sentence: China banned importing seafood from Japan.
correction: China banned imports of seafood from Japan. / China banned the import of seafood from Japan.
explanation: The teacher recast the sentence with the noun phrase "imports of seafood." Use "ban imports" or "ban the import of..." for a government trade restriction.
correct_examples: China banned imports of seafood from Japan.
The country banned the import of that product.

my_sentence: Jinhua belong to Zhejiang.
correction: Jinhua belongs to Zhejiang Province.
explanation: The singular subject "Jinhua" needs the third-person singular verb "belongs."
correct_examples: Jinhua belongs to Zhejiang Province.
This city belongs to the region.
context: Describing the location of fish farms.

my_sentence: My country import oil from Iran.
correction: My country imports oil from Iran.
explanation: The singular subject "my country" requires "imports" in the simple present.
correct_examples: My country imports oil from Iran.
The company imports fuel.

my_sentence: Most of the countries who produce oil.
correction: In most of the countries that produce oil.
explanation: The sentence needed the opening preposition "in." For countries, the teacher also changed the relative pronoun from "who" to "that."
correct_examples: Oil is important in most countries that produce it.
Prices rose in the countries that export fuel.

my_sentence: I can't remember this country's name.
correction: I can't remember the country's name.
explanation: Use "the country" when referring to a particular country already being discussed; its name is expressed with the possessive "country's."
correct_examples: I can't remember the country's name.
Do you know the company's name?
context: Trying to name another oil-producing country.

my_sentence: If you want to build a store such as KFC store...
correction: If you want to open a store such as a KFC restaurant...
explanation: A singular countable example needs an article: "a KFC restaurant." "Open a store" is also more natural than "build a store" when discussing starting a business.
correct_examples: She wants to open a KFC restaurant.
They plan to open a small store.
context: Discussing the cost of a franchise.

my_sentence: When you meet a problem...
correction: When you encounter a problem...
explanation: Use the collocation "encounter a problem," not "meet a problem." This recurring L1-influenced pattern was also corrected in earlier lessons.
correct_examples: Call me if you encounter a problem.
We encountered several technical issues.

my_sentence: If a person build connections with different people...
correction: If a person builds connections with different people...
explanation: The singular subject "a person" takes the third-person singular verb "builds." The teacher explicitly prompted for the singular form.
correct_examples: If a person builds connections, opportunities follow.
Someone who works hard improves.

my_sentence: He or she will have more business opportunity.
correction: He or she will have more business opportunities.
explanation: After "more," a countable noun normally takes the plural form: "opportunities."
correct_examples: She will have more business opportunities.
Networking creates new opportunities.

my_sentence: They can share their knowledge and information to each other.
correction: They can share their knowledge and information with each other.
explanation: The verb "share" takes "with" for the people receiving or exchanging something.
correct_examples: They share information with each other.
I shared the report with my manager.

my_sentence: I will familiar with the employees in my company.
correction: I am more familiar with the employees in my company.
explanation: "Familiar" is an adjective, so it needs a form of "be." The teacher corrected it to "I am more familiar."
correct_examples: I am familiar with this system.
She is more familiar with the process.
context: Using internal connections to learn company plans.

my_sentence: I need to stay the company or stay other company.
correction: I need to stay in the company or work for another company.
explanation: "Stay" needs "in" before a place or organization. When changing employers, "work for another company" is the natural expression.
correct_examples: I may stay in the company.
He wants to work for another company.

my_sentence: Even though I will find a new job, I still in the retail industry.
correction: Even if I find a new job, I will still stay in the retail industry.
explanation: The main clause needs a verb: "will still stay." Use present tense after "even if" when describing a possible future condition.
correct_examples: Even if I change jobs, I will stay in retail.
I will still work in the same industry.
context: Explaining why he would remain in retail.

my_sentence: I don't want to start all again.
correction: I don't want to start all over again. / I don't want to start anew.
explanation: The fixed expression is "start all over again." The teacher also supplied the concise alternative "start anew."
correct_examples: I don't want to start all over again.
Moving industries would mean starting anew.

my_sentence: If they can provide a higher salary, I can consider.
correction: If they can provide a higher salary, I can consider it.
explanation: "Consider" is transitive here and needs an object. The teacher prompted and Jack repaired it to "consider it."
correct_examples: If the salary is higher, I can consider it.
We will consider the offer.
context: Conditions for moving to another industry.

my_sentence: I prefer to financial industries.
correction: I prefer to go into the financial industry.
explanation: "Prefer" cannot be followed directly by a destination noun with "to." Use an infinitive such as "prefer to go into."
correct_examples: I prefer to go into the financial industry.
She prefers working in retail.
context: Choosing another sector to work in.

my_sentence: They more care about security.
correction: They care more about security.
explanation: With the verb phrase "care about," the comparative adverb "more" comes after the verb: "care more about." The teacher recast this order several times.
correct_examples: Banks care more about security.
Customers care more about reliability.
context: Why financial companies invest in infrastructure.

my_sentence: If they compare to technology companies, they can't earn more than that.
correction: If they are compared with technology companies, they earn less.
explanation: The companies are being compared, so a passive form is needed: "are compared with." Use "earn less" for the direct comparison.
correct_examples: If restaurants are compared with tech companies, they earn less.
Compared with large firms, this shop earns less.

my_sentence: You need to join some meeting to get many useful information.
correction: You need to join some meetings to get some useful information.
explanation: "Some meetings" needs a plural countable noun. "Information" is uncountable, so use "some information," not "many information."
correct_examples: Join some meetings to get useful information.
We received a lot of useful information.

my_sentence: For me, it's not a big question.
correction: For me, it's not a big problem.
explanation: Use "problem" for a difficulty or concern. A "question" is something asked in order to get an answer.
correct_examples: The price increase is not a big problem for me.
I have a question about the price.
context: Responding to the teacher's price increase.

my_sentence: I think there economy is better than us.
correction: I think their economy is better than ours.
explanation: Use the possessive determiner "their" before "economy" and the possessive pronoun "ours" after "than" to compare economies.
correct_examples: Their economy is stronger than ours.
Their salaries are higher than ours.

my_sentence: We are not have an interaction.
correction: We will not have any interactions with each other.
explanation: Do not put "are" before the base verb "have." For a future prediction, use "will not have." The general relationship is naturally plural: "interactions."
correct_examples: We will not have any interactions with them.
The two industries do not interact.
context: Explaining why his company does not need entertainment-industry data.

my_sentence: Sometimes you will meet same problem.
correction: Sometimes you will encounter the same problem.
explanation: The fixed phrase is "the same problem." Use the natural collocation "encounter a problem" rather than "meet a problem."
correct_examples: Sometimes competitors encounter the same problem.
We faced the same issue last week.
context: How business connections help solve problems.

my_sentence: Maybe they met this question before.
correction: Maybe they have encountered this problem before.
explanation: Use the present perfect with "before" when the past experience matters now: "have encountered." A technical difficulty is a "problem," not a "question."
correct_examples: They have encountered this problem before.
Our team has seen this issue before.
context: Asking competitors for help with a device problem.

my_sentence: They can give you some recommendation.
correction: They can recommend something to you. / They can give you some recommendations.
explanation: Use the verb pattern "recommend something to someone," or use the plural count noun "recommendations" after "some."
correct_examples: They can recommend a solution to you.
They gave us some recommendations.

my_sentence: This can last in a long time.
correction: This can last for a long time.
explanation: Use "for" to express a duration: "for a long time."
correct_examples: This partnership can last for a long time.
The meeting lasted for two hours.
context: Whether business connections have long-term value.

my_sentence: They can easily become cooperator.
correction: They can easily cooperate.
explanation: After the modal "can," use the base verb "cooperate." "Cooperator" is a noun and would also need an article or plural ending.
correct_examples: The two families can easily cooperate.
They became business partners.

my_sentence: It help them become business partners.
correction: It helps them become business partners.
explanation: The singular subject "it" takes "helps" in the simple present. After "help," the bare infinitive "become" is correct.
correct_examples: It helps them become partners.
Networking helps a company grow.
